The recommendation's stated benefit ('site/Expo use the same component with minimal platform detection') is hypothetical — nobody uses this component cross-platform today. This is P3, not P2.

2) Recommendation partly unsound. 'Adopt the web children+refs interface as canonical for native' cannot work: the native overlay technique structurally requires the component to own the TextInput (it sets the real input text color to transparent at native.tsx:64 and layers a colored Text overlay at lines 92-109). The web hover model (hoveredTokenIdx, getBoundingClientRect, onMouseMove/Leave) is DOM/mouse-only with no RN equivalent. The fallback render-prop design (caller owns the TextInput) also breaks the transparent-input technique. So 'unify the interface' is not clearly correct — these may legitimately be platform-specific siblings sharing only a name. A sound, cheaper fix: keep them separate but give distinct exported type names (drop the misleading 'as TokenOverlayInputProps' alias) and document in dice-ui CLAUDE.md that the variants are intentionally non-isomorphic. Effort S, not M.

Bottom line: the prop mismatch is real and current and worth a small cleanup, but it is lower severity (P3) and the prescribed unification is partly infeasible.

survivedFindings.title: No type-safety tests for multi-target consistency (export parity, prop parity, logic equivalence)
survivedFindings.problem: The single test file covers only tokenColor utility. There are no tests for: (1) that index.ts and index.native.ts export the same symbols (DocModal is already missing from one), (2) that component props match between .tsx and .native.tsx, (3) that the rendering logic for DieBadge, StepRow, RollSteps produces equivalent DOM/tree structure on both targets. A refactor to unify code could silently break the native variant, and vice versa.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add a test suite that (a) compares export lists between barrels, (b) validates prop interface parity via TypeScript type tests, (c) spot-checks rendering equivalence (e.g., DieBadge with variant='added' renders identically). Use a snapshot or structural assertion approach to catch drift.

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED and stronger than reported. The export asymmetry is real: packages/dice-ui/src/index.ts:13 exports `DocModal` (and index.ts:2 exports `DocModalProps`), but packages/dice-ui/src/index.native.ts:14 only exports `QuickReferenceGrid` — `DocModal` is NOT re-exported on native. In QuickReferenceGrid.native.tsx:502 `DocModal` is a local non-exported `function`, whereas in the web QuickReferenceGrid.tsx:616 it is `export function DocModal`.

This is not just a hypothetical the finding frames it as ("a refactor COULD silently break native"). It is a LIVE runtime bug on the native target: apps/expo/app/index.tsx:3 does `import { DocModal } from '@randsum/dice-ui'`; apps/expo/metro.config.js:40 resolves native to `src/index.native.ts`, which does not export DocModal — so DocModal is `undefined` at runtime, and rendering `<DocModal .../>` at index.tsx:281 crashes React Native with "Element type is invalid... got: undefined." Two things mask it: (1) the hand-written ambient declaration apps/expo/types/dice-ui.d.ts:43 declares `DocModal` exists, so `tsc` passes; (2) the test mock IndexScreen.test.tsx:111 hard-codes `DocModal: 'DocModal'`, so tests never hit the real barrel. Web is fine (web barrel exports it). Blast radius is contained: package is `private: true`, consumed only by apps/expo and apps/site.

The finding's recommendation (export-parity assertion between the two barrels, plus prop/render parity) is sound and non-duplicative — __tests__/ contains only tokenColor.test.ts, and an export-list diff between index.ts and index.native.ts would have caught this exact bug. 

Adjustment: the finding mis-frames a shipped native crash as a P2 "we lack parity tests / expand" gap. The generic test-investment is fairly P2, but because it is masking a current runtime defect in the native expo build, the actionable severity is P1: fix the missing native export (re-export DocModal from index.native.ts and/or export it from QuickReferenceGrid.native.tsx) AND add the export-parity test that guards it going forward. Also worth deleting the redundant hand-written apps/expo/types/dice-ui.d.ts which actively hides drift like this.

survivedFindings.title: QuickReferenceGrid complexity (1050 LOC web, 866 native) — unclear if dual-maintenance is worth the native support
survivedFindings.problem: QuickReferenceGrid is the largest component by far, implementing a complex notation reference with modal, category grouping, filtering, and builder-type UI. The web version uses CSS grid + HTML + inline styles; the native version uses React Native's SectionList + StyleSheet. Both are ~1000 LOC and share zero implementation (can't extract even the groupByCategory logic because it's duplicated). The Expo app uses it heavily, but if native performance issues arose, the entire 1916 LOC would need rework. The component is also rarely touched — it shipped in commit dda5b5ff and has had only bug fixes / color contrast since.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Either (1) commit to native and refactor to extract all logic, share category grouping, merge prop interfaces, or (2) gate QuickReferenceGrid to web only and render it via 'use dom' WebView on native. The current state (two full parallel implementations with no shared logic) is a maintenance anti-pattern. If Expo never hits performance issues with the WebView variant, dropping native saves 1000 LOC and eliminates a code-review burden. This decision should be explicit and documented in CLAUDE.md.

verdict.reasoning: VERIFIED REAL, but the finding mis-diagnoses the situation. Confirmed facts: LOC exact (1050 web / 866 native / 1916 total via wc -l); QuickReferenceGrid is the largest dice-ui component by 4x (next largest is NotationRoller at ~245 LOC each); the two files share ZERO imports/implementation; and it was NOT covered in the prior 30-item audit (grep of audit/ + plans/ found nothing). The duplication is real and concrete: groupByCategory() is BYTE-FOR-BYTE identical between packages/dice-ui/src/QuickReferenceGrid.tsx (line 83) and .native.tsx (line 113), and NUMBER_KEYS, CONDITION_KEYS, DICE_SIDES_KEYS, CATEGORY_ORDER, and OPERATORS are all duplicated verbatim. Git history confirms only incremental UI polish (a11y, layout) since introduction.\n\nThree corrections drive the 'adjusted' verdict: (1) Wrong commit hash — finding cites dda5b5ff but the actual introducing commit is 6602d5af. (2) The claim 'can't extract even the groupByCategory logic because it's duplicated' is FALSE — it is byte-identical and trivially extractable to a shared module; duplication is the symptom, not a barrier. (3) Most importantly, the recommendation's option-2 ('gate to web only, render via use dom WebView on native') and its closing line ('this decision should be explicit and documented in CLAUDE.md') are already done. apps/expo/docs/adrs/ADR-003-dice-ui-integration-via-use-dom.md and apps/expo/CLAUDE.md both document the exact three-step strategy: web=direct, native=use-dom WebView (default escape hatch), and '.native.tsx port ONLY if WebView perf is a blocker — explicitly NOT built in the initial sprint.' So QuickReferenceGrid.native.tsx existing at all is the project already VIOLATING its own documented ADR by jumping to step 3 without the documented perf-blocker trigger. The finding's strategic instinct is correct (1000 LOC of unjustified dual-maintenance), but it frames this as an open undocumented decision when it's a documented decision the code contradicts. Secondary real issue it surfaces: packages/dice-ui/CLAUDE.md is badly stale — it documents none of the .native.tsx variants, omits QuickReferenceGrid entirely, lists useTheme.ts (actually .tsx) and a non-existent RollResultDisplay export. Severity P2 is appropriate (real maintenance/strategic debt, non-blocking, private package). Recommended action: either delete QuickReferenceGrid.native.tsx and rely on the documented use-dom path, OR if native is intentionally kept, extract the identical helpers to a shared module and update both dice-ui/CLAUDE.md and ADR-003 to record the deviation.

survivedFindings.title: Metro resolver documentation incomplete: missing guidance on adding new subpaths
survivedFindings.problem: CLAUDE.md states 'All workspace subpaths are enumerated explicitly in the resolveRequest function. If a new subpath is added to @randsum/roller or @randsum/dice-ui, it must be added to this map.' But there's no description of HOW to add one or WHAT the map looks like. A developer adding a new game package subpath (e.g., @randsum/games/planescape) would have to reverse-engineer metro.config.js. The config is also untyped and runs before TypeScript, so errors surface only at runtime.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add a code comment in metro.config.js showing the pattern (e.g., `'@randsum/games/planescape': resolve(__dirname, '../../packages/games/src/planescape.ts')`) and reference it from CLAUDE.md with a section 'Adding a new workspace subpath.' Consider adding a script (e.g., `scripts/add-workspace-subpath.ts`) that patches metro.config.js and validates the result.

verdict.reasoning:
Files verified. The resolver map is real and manually maintained at apps/expo/metro.config.js (resolveRequest, ~lines 29-45), untyped JS, and a missing entry surfaces only at runtime via the context.resolveRequest fallback. So the core mechanism exists.

But the finding is significantly overstated and mis-framed:

1. INVALID CENTRAL EXAMPLE: "@randsum/games/planescape" cannot happen. apps/expo/package.json depends ONLY on @randsum/roller and @randsum/dice-ui (verified). The map covers only roller/dice-ui subpaths; a games subpath would never be added here. The finding's own example reflects a misunderstanding of the map's scope.

2. OVERSTATED "reverse-engineer" CLAIM: apps/expo/CLAUDE.md already has a dedicated "Metro Resolver — Load-Bearing" section with a "Workspace TypeScript source resolution" subsection that explicitly states new subpaths must be added, documents the platform fork for dice-ui, and warns to verify with `bun run web` + a native build. The map is self-documenting — each entry is a literal `'subpath': path.resolve(monorepoRoot, 'packages/.../src/X.ts')` that can be copied. This is not "reverse-engineering."

3. UNSOUND HALF OF RECOMMENDATION: adding a scripts/add-workspace-subpath.ts that patches metro.config.js is over-engineering for a private app with ~8 stable entries that change rarely — the kind of dead-weight tooling this audit should flag for removal, not addition.

The only genuinely actionable kernel is trivial: add an inline comment in metro.config.js showing the one-line pattern. That is a P3 nicety, not a P2 DX gap. Adjusting to P3 and scoping the recommendation to just the inline comment.

survivedFindings.title: CLI lacks game-specific roll commands; limits adoption for actual gaming
survivedFindings.problem: The CLI only accepts raw notation strings (e.g. `randsum 4d6L`). Users cannot roll game-specific mechanics like D&D 5e ability scores, Blades in the Dark resistance rolls, or any named roll from the game packages. The 6 game packages (@randsum/games subpaths) are entirely inaccessible from the CLI, forcing users to either use the web playground or calculate notation themselves.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add game-specific subcommands or a `--game` flag that exposes the game packages' named rolls. Example: `randsum fifth ability-score` or `randsum --game=fifth ability-score`. This would unlock the full value of the ecosystem and dramatically improve usability for the target audience (TTRPG GMs/players). Alternatively, document this limitation and recommend the web playground or Discord bot for game-specific rolls.

verdict.reasoning:
The factual premise is CONFIRMED against current code. apps/cli/src/index.ts (verified) is a flat notation parser — it only collects notation strings, routes them through runRolls()/@randsum/roller, and exposes no game access. apps/cli/package.json lists only @randsum/roller (devDep, bundled); none of the @randsum/games subpaths are imported. apps/cli/README.md documents only notation usage. So "the 6 game packages are inaccessible from the CLI" is accurate and current. It is also non-duplicative: prior audits (audit/dimensions/apps.md F1/F2, plans/3.0.0-harsh-audit-round-2/3) covered the phantom -i flag, stdin/exit codes (now fixed — index.ts has readStdinSync + process.exit(1)), and packaging/workspace-dep issues — none proposed exposing game packages.

However the finding is MIS-RATED at P1 and its mechanism is partly inaccurate:

1. Severity overstated. This is a feature-expansion opinion, not a defect. The CLI works correctly for its declared scope — apps/cli/CLAUDE.md states "Simple terminal dice roller. Pass notation args, get results." There is no broken behavior, no incorrect output, no user-trust damage (contrast the real P1 in prior audit, the phantom -i flag, which IS trust damage). Per the project's own strategy (MEMORY: Discord bot #939 and the Expo/web playground are the channels for richer interaction; the "Unified Roller App Sprint" planned an Ink TUI that has since been deliberately stripped back out — index.ts no longer has src/tui or an Ink dep), keeping the CLI notation-only looks intentional, not an oversight. This belongs at P2 (a worthwhile "expand" idea) or even P3.

2. Mechanism inaccurate. The recommendation assumes "named rolls" like `randsum fifth ability-score`. Verified packages/games/src/fifth.generated.ts: each game subpath exports a SINGLE roll(input) function (fifth takes {modifier, rollingWith, crit}), not a catalog of named rolls. So the proposed subcommand surface doesn't map onto the actual game API and would need design work the finding doesn't acknowledge.

The finding's better/secondary recommendation — document the scope boundary and point users to the playground/Discord bot for game rolls — is the cheaper, more defensible move and is closer to a CLARIFY than an EXPAND. Real and actionable, but P2, not P1.

survivedFindings.title: Verbose output lacks visual separation and per-roll subtotals for multi-notation rolls
survivedFindings.problem: When rolling multiple notations (e.g. `randsum 2d6 4d6L 1d20+5 --verbose`), the output shows all rolls sequentially without clear separation. Each roll's section lacks a per-roll subtotal, making it hard to see which total applies to which notation. The final `Total:` line is a sum but doesn't clarify whether each notation contributes equally or if some have modifiers.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Improve formatVerbose to add (1) blank lines or dashes between roll sections, (2) show `Subtotal:` for each record using `record.total`, (3) optionally label each roll with its index or notation. Example: '--- Roll 1: 2d6 --- / Roll: ... / Subtotal: 8 / --- Roll 2: 4d6L --- / Roll: ... / Subtotal: 14 / Total: 22'

verdict.reasoning: Verified against actual code and runtime output. /Users/jarvis/Code/RANDSUM/@RANDSUM/apps/cli/src/format.ts:19-34 (formatVerbose) loops over result.rolls pushing only Roll:/Raw:/Kept: lines per record, then a single `Total:` at the end — no separators between sections and no per-roll subtotal. Confirmed by running `randsum 2d6 4d6L 1d20+5 --verbose --seed 42`, which produced 9 run-together lines ending in `Total: 26` with no way to attribute portions to each notation (3 + 9 + 14 = 26 is non-obvious). Location is accurate. The recommendation is sound and implementable: RollRecord.total exists (packages/roller/src/types/results.ts:62) and is already consumed by formatJson in the same file, so adding a `Subtotal:` line and a divider is trivial. Not a duplicate — no prior audit item (audit/, plans/3.0.0-harsh-audit*.md) mentions verbose output, subtotals, or CLI formatting. One nuance strengthening the finding: verbose mode also hides arithmetic (1d20+5 shows Raw [9]/Kept [9] with no visible +5), which compounds the reconciliation difficulty the finding describes. Severity P2 / category refine / effort S are all appropriate: a real but low-stakes UX roughness in a published-but-low-traffic CLI, not a correctness bug.

survivedFindings.title: Compact format output is visually ambiguous for multi-notation rolls
survivedFindings.problem: Compact output for `randsum 1d20+5 2d6` produces `26  [16]  Add 5  [4, 1]`, which is hard to parse at a glance. It's unclear where one roll ends and another begins. The array `[16]` could be misread as a single die value, and the `[4, 1]` appears appended without clear grouping.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Improve compact format to use separators or clearer grouping. Example: `26 = [16]+5 + [4,1]` or `26 {1d20+5: 21} {2d6: 5}`. Alternatively, always use JSON for machine-readable output and reserve compact/verbose for human readability of single rolls only.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the real code. `apps/cli/src/format.ts:3-17` (formatCompact) exists exactly as cited and joins all parts with double-spaces via `parts.join('  ')`, pushing `[rolls]` then trailing modifier descriptions per record with no per-notation grouping. Reproduced live: `randsum 1d20+5 2d6 -s 42` outputs `16  [6]  Add 5  [1, 4]`. With multiple notations the boundary is genuinely ambiguous — `[1, 4]` reads as another modifier appended to roll 1 rather than a separate dice pool, exactly as the finding claims (the finding's `26 [16] Add 5 [4,1]` example is illustrative; structure matches reality). The problem is real and current.

Not duplicative: prior audits (audit/synthesis-top-30.md, plans/3.0.0-harsh-audit-round-3.md) flag other CLI issues (phantom `-i` README flag, `catalog:`/`workspace:~` publish bugs, stdin support) but none touch compact-output legibility. Notably the prior audit inventory still describes an Ink-based TUI CLI that no longer exists — the CLI was rewritten to these plain-text formatters, so this code is under-reviewed and the finding is genuinely new.

Recommendation is partially sound: the separator/grouping idea is correct and low-risk (CLI tests only assert `/^\d+/` on compact output, so a separator won't break them). But the alternative — "reserve compact for single rolls, force JSON for machine output" — is misguided; it would remove the legitimate multi-notation compact use case rather than improve it. Keep only the separator half.

Severity: P2 is slightly generous. This is a cosmetic, single-app, human-readability rough edge on default output; it never yields wrong values, and `-v` verbose mode already gives unambiguous per-roll breakdown. P3 is the right rating. Confirming the issue but adjusting severity down to P3.

survivedFindings.title: No size-limit constraint on CLI package; 256KB binary is unaudited
survivedFindings.problem: All other publishable packages (roller 16KB, games 15KB each, salvageunion 35KB, component-library) have `size-limit` entries in their package.json. The CLI has 256KB dist output (92KB index.js + sourcemap) with no configured size gate. A future bundler misconfiguration or accidental heavy dependency could ship without notice.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add a `size-limit` entry to apps/cli/package.json capping dist/index.js at ~100KB (current 92KB plus buffer). This enforces the thin one-shot binary constraint. Verify the CLI is included in the root `bun run size` validation step.

verdict.reasoning:
The core problem is real and current. Verified at /Users/jarvis/Code/RANDSUM/@RANDSUM/apps/cli/package.json: the CLI is published ("private": false, has bin/main/files for npm), ships dist/index.js at 94KB (plus a 166KB .map, hence the ~256KB total cited), and has NO size-limit config. Only two packages have size-limit: packages/roller/package.json (16KB) and packages/games/package.json. The CI gate in .github/workflows/publish.yml:32 explicitly runs `size` ONLY on roller and games (`bun run --filter '@randsum/roller' size && bun run --filter '@randsum/games' size`), so the CLI binary genuinely ships unaudited. A future heavy-dep mistake would pass undetected. Not a duplicate of the prior audits (audit/3.0.0-harsh-audit*.md mention salvageunion size and CLI workspace-dep, but never the missing CLI size gate).

However, the finding's supporting details contain factual errors that need correction (not enough to reject, since the central claim and location hold):
1. It claims "games 15KB each, salvageunion 35KB, component-library" have size-limit entries. False. There is no standalone salvageunion package (it's a games subpath) and no component-library package (the UI package is packages/dice-ui, also without size-limit). Only roller and games carry size-limit.
2. The recommendation says to "verify the CLI is included in the root `bun run size`" — but NO root `size` script exists. `check:all` just calls `check`, which runs per-package check scripts, and none of those (including roller/games) include `size`. The only place size runs in CI is publish.yml.

So the correct fix is two-part: add a `size-limit` entry + `size` script to apps/cli/package.json capping dist/index.js (~100KB given current 94KB), AND add the CLI filter to the size step in .github/workflows/publish.yml:32 — otherwise the new gate would never run. P2/clarify/S rating is sound (CI/DX hardening, no user-facing breakage, small effort).

survivedFindings.title: No usage examples for stdin/pipe integration in README; feature is undiscovered
survivedFindings.problem: The audit fixed stdin support (PR #1056), but the README doesn't document it. Users won't know they can pipe notation: `echo '4d6L' | randsum` or chain commands: `randsum 4d6L --json | jq '.total'`. The feature exists but is invisible to discovery.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add a 'Piping' or 'Integration' section to the README with examples: `echo '4d6L' | randsum`, `randsum 4d6L --json | jq '.total'`, `for roll in {1..6}; do randsum 4d6L; done | awk '{s+=$1} END {print s}'`. Mention that stdin is read when no args are passed and stdin is not a TTY.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against actual code. The stdin/pipe feature is real and current: apps/cli/src/index.ts lines 104-108 read stdin via readStdinSync() when no notation args are passed and process.stdin.isTTY is false. PR #1056 shipped this (prior audit item #25 / [apps:F2] fixed the implementation and explicitly cited the `randsum 4d6L --json | jq '.total'` use case). However, apps/cli/README.md (full file read) contains ZERO mention of stdin, piping, jq, or chaining — grep for those terms returns exit 1. So the feature is genuinely invisible to discovery.

The recommended examples are accurate against the actual output formats: apps/cli/src/format.ts line 38 emits a `.total` field in JSON output (so `| jq '.total'` works), and line 4 puts `String(result.total)` as the first token of compact output (so the awk-sum example works too).

This is NOT a duplicate of prior audit item #25 — that item fixed the implementation + exit codes; this is the follow-up documentation gap, which the prior audit did not call out. It is also distinct from prior item #14 (the phantom `-i`/`--interactive` flag), which the current README no longer contains.

Severity P2 is correct: this is a 'clarify'/DX discoverability issue with no correctness impact — the feature works regardless. Effort S is correct (a few README lines). Location apps/cli/README.md is accurate. Recommendation is sound and non-duplicative.

survivedFindings.title: Help text example `2d20L +5` is invalid syntax; will confuse users
survivedFindings.problem: The help example shows `randsum 2d20L +5` without quotes. In shell, this parses as two separate arguments: `2d20L` (valid) and `+5` (invalid notation). Users copying this literally will get errors. Correct usage requires quoting: `randsum '2d20L+5'` or concatenation without space.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Update the help example to `randsum '2d20L+5'` (with quotes) or `randsum 2d20L+5` (no space). Add a note in the Examples section: 'Complex notation with spaces must be quoted: randsum "2d20L + 5"'.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified directly against apps/cli/src/index.ts. Line 23 of the HELP constant contains the example `randsum 2d20L +5` (labeled "Roll with advantage + modifier"). Running this literal command (bun run dev -- 2d20L +5) produces `Error: Validation error: "+5" is not valid dice notation` and exits non-zero. The quoted form `randsum '2d20L+5'` works correctly (outputs "25 [20] Drop lowest, Add 5"), confirming the recommended fix is sound. The finding is NEW: the prior audit (audit/dimensions/apps.md) only references 2d20L in the context of the missing -i interactive flag drift, not this help-text syntax issue, so it is non-duplicative.

One nuance: the finding's *mechanism* is mildly inaccurate. It attributes the error to shell-level arg splitting where `+5` is "invalid notation" standalone. In reality, run.ts passes ALL parsed notations together as roll('2d20L', '+5') — the roller's multi-argument API requires each argument to be self-contained notation (e.g. roll('1d20+5','2d6')), and a bare '+5' fails that per-argument validation. The outcome (example errors) and fix (quote/concatenate) are correct regardless, so this does not change the verdict, only the explanation.

P2 is appropriate: a genuine copy-paste failure in the primary --help output of a published npm CLI, but cosmetic with low blast radius. Confirmed and actionable.

survivedFindings.title: No documentation for output formats or their use cases
survivedFindings.problem: The README lists three output modes (`-v/--verbose`, `--json`) but doesn't explain when to use each, what they contain, or why someone would choose one over another. Compact is the default but never named. Users must reverse-engineer the format difference.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add a 'Output Formats' section to the README explaining: compact (default, one-liner for quick rolls), verbose (multi-line detail with Raw/Kept/Total), json (machine-readable for scripting). Include one example of each and note when to use each format (quick rolls vs detail vs scripting).

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the actual files. apps/cli/README.md (Options section) lists `-v/--verbose` and `--json` with one-line descriptions but never explains what each contains, when to choose one, or names the default (compact) format. apps/cli/src/format.ts confirms three distinct formatters exist: formatCompact (total + bracketed rolls), formatVerbose (multi-line Roll/Raw/Kept/Total), and formatJson (total, rolls[].{description,raw,kept,total}). The README gives no use-case guidance for any of them.

Not duplicative: the prior audit (audit/dimensions/apps.md) touched the CLI README only for a phantom `-i/--interactive` flag (F1, now removed) and the conformance-version claim (F8, now showing v0.9.0). Neither addressed output-format documentation. So this is a genuinely-open, distinct finding.

The finding has one minor wording imprecision: it says the README lists 'three output modes' then notes compact is never named — the README actually documents only two flags, with compact implicit as default. This doesn't undermine the core claim; the finding's own problem statement acknowledges compact is the unnamed default. The recommendation is sound and proportionate.

Severity P3 is correct: this is pure DX polish on a published CLI, not a correctness or user-trust break (unlike the phantom flag, which was P1). Effort S is reasonable for a README section with three examples.

survivedFindings.title: Seeded random semantics are undocumented; users may not understand when to use --seed
survivedFindings.problem: The `--seed N` flag uses a deterministic LCG. The help text doesn't explain the difference between seeded (same result every time) vs unseeded (random). Users unfamiliar with RNG concepts won't understand the tradeoff or when --seed is useful.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Expand the help text for `--seed` to: `--seed N   Set random seed for reproducible rolls (same result every time). Without --seed, uses system RNG.` This clarifies the difference. Don't add cryptographic or OS RNG options unless there's demand; the current setup is sufficient for the CLI's scope.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against actual code. The LCG-based seeded random exists at apps/cli/src/run.ts:19-28 (createSeededRandom). The in-CLI help text at index.ts:17 is terse ("-s, --seed N  Use seeded random") with no explanation of what seeding does or when to use it. The README at README.md:39 is marginally clearer ("Set random seed for reproducible rolls") but still doesn't explain the seeded-vs-unseeded tradeoff. The finding's facts, locations, and line numbers are all accurate. The recommendation is sound, non-duplicative (no existing code/doc explains the tradeoff), and appropriately scoped (correctly resists adding crypto/OS RNG options). This is a genuine but minor documentation-clarity gap: --seed is an opt-in advanced flag, the README already conveys "reproducible," and users who reach for seeding generally understand RNG concepts — so the actual cost is low. P3 (lowest tier) is the correct severity for a docs-polish nitpick. It is actionable (one-line help text edit). Not duplicative of the prior 30-item audit, which focused on publish/build/correctness P0-P2 items.

survivedFindings.title: Test suite doesn't cover edge cases or malformed input
survivedFindings.problem: Tests cover happy path and basic errors. Edge cases like `--repeat abc` (non-numeric), very large pools (e.g. `10000d10000`), or conflicting flags are not tested. Flag parsing is vulnerable to edge cases or undocumented behavior.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add tests for: (1) `--repeat` with non-numeric value (current behavior: defaults to 1), (2) very large pool sizes (should not hang or overflow), (3) conflicting flags like `--json --verbose` (document which takes precedence), (4) stdin edge cases (empty, binary data). Keep scope focused on CLI-specific behavior, not roller robustness.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed the cited files exist (apps/cli/__tests__/run.test.ts, index.test.ts) and that they only cover happy-path + basic notation errors. The finding's core premise — flag parsing has untested, surprising edge cases — is REAL and verified by running the CLI. However, the finding's stated current behavior is factually WRONG, so I'm marking it adjusted rather than confirmed.

Actual behavior (verified by execution):
- `randsum 1d6 --repeat abc` does NOT "default to 1". The skip-logic in apps/cli/src/index.ts:64-71 only consumes a flag's value when it parses as a number (`!Number.isNaN(Number(arg))`), so the non-numeric `abc` leaks through to `notations.push(arg)` (line 74) and produces "Validation error: \"abc\" is not valid dice notation" + exit 1. Same latent bug affects `--seed abc`.
- `--repeat 0`: `Number('0') || 1` (index.ts:56) hits the falsy trap and silently rolls once instead of zero times.
- `--repeat -5`: `Array.from({ length: -5 })` (run.ts:41) yields [], so the CLI silently produces no output and exits 0 — a confusing no-op.
- `--json --verbose`: json wins (run.ts:34 ternary), undocumented precedence.

These confirm the "clarify"/undocumented-behavior framing. The recommendation to add CLI-specific edge-case tests is sound and non-duplicative — no existing test touches any of these, and the prior audit only covered stdin/exit-code (synthesis F2, already implemented in current index.ts at lines 104-113/132). 

Two parts of the recommendation are weak: (1) the "very large pools (10000d10000) should not hang/overflow" item is unfounded — I ran it and it completes instantly with correct output; (2) stdin edge cases are partially covered by the already-shipped F2 work. The real value is testing/fixing the `--repeat`/`--seed` argument-consumption bug and documenting flag precedence. Severity P3 is correct (robustness/UX polish, no crash or data corruption); the parser bug is a genuine but minor defect. Effort is closer to S (tests) or S–M (tests + parser guards) than M.

survivedFindings.title: Five apps have nearly identical script patterns but no shared base or DRY abstraction
survivedFindings.problem: Each app duplicates the same 6-8 scripts verbatim (build, typecheck, format, lint, test, check). Changes to linting rules or TypeScript config require manual edits in 4-5 places. The root package.json has a corresponding check script that chains them serially (8 subprocess invocations for one CI gate). This fragmentation makes it harder to keep CI gates consistent and makes mass-refactors require scatter-gun edits.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Create a shared scripts.json or Makefile that's imported/generated in each app's package.json, or adopt a monorepo task runner abstraction. At minimum, document the canonical script set in root CLAUDE.md and add a linting rule that flags check script deviations.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all five app package.jsons. The core observation (duplicated script strings) is real but the finding is overstated and partly inaccurate, warranting a downgrade from P2 to P3.

WHAT'S TRUE: `format` and `format:check` are byte-identical across all 5 apps; `lint` is identical across cli/discord-bot/rdn/site; the `check` chain `bun run build && bun run typecheck && bun run format:check && bun run lint && bun run test` is verbatim in cli, discord-bot, rdn, site. So duplication of these strings genuinely exists. This is also a NEW finding — the prior audit (audit/dimensions/build-tooling-ci.md, apps.md) covers TS version fragmentation (F4) and redundant CI builds (F10) but NOT script duplication.

WHAT'S INACCURATE: (1) "Five apps have nearly identical patterns" is false — apps/expo/package.json has NO `check`, NO `test`, and NO `build` script (it uses `expo start`/`bunup`-less Expo tooling) and a different `lint` (`eslint app hooks lib __tests__`). Only 4 apps share the check pattern. (2) "8 subprocess invocations" is wrong — root `check` chains 6 package filters (roller, games, cli, discord-bot, site, rdn), each internally chaining 5 steps.

WHY IMPACT IS LOW: The duplicated script lines all delegate to SHARED root configs (`-c ../../eslint.config.js`, `--config ../../.prettierrc`, `tsc` reads inherited tsconfig). So a lint/format rule change happens in the shared config, NOT in each script string — the "scatter-gun edit" risk the finding asserts barely materializes. Moreover the root package.json ALREADY implements the recommended abstraction for lint/format/typecheck via `bun run --filter '@randsum/*' lint`, so part of the fix exists.

RECOMMENDATION FLAW: "shared scripts.json or Makefile that's imported/generated in each app's package.json" is partly infeasible — package.json `scripts` cannot import from an external file; npm/bun have no such mechanism. The realistic fix is the existing `--filter` pattern plus documenting a canonical script set, which is minor.

Net: real, current, correctly located, non-duplicative — but cosmetic with near-zero maintenance cost and factual errors. Adjust P2 -> P3.

survivedFindings.title: Discord-bot has 6 game commands that follow 90% identical structure: parse args -> build embed -> catch error -> reply
survivedFindings.problem: Each command independently: (1) imports game package, (2) defines buildXyzEmbed(params), (3) exports Command with SlashCommandBuilder + async execute, (4) wraps roll() in try/catch. The embed building is game-specific, but the command harness and error handling are pure boilerplate. A bug fix in the command structure requires edits to 6+ files. Adding a new game requires copy-pasting a full 100-line file.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Extract a createGameCommand(gameImportFn, buildEmbed, slashCommandConfig) factory function. This reduces each game command to ~30 lines: import, define params, build embed, call factory. Maintains game-specific logic while eliminating harness duplication.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed real and current. All 6 game commands (blades.ts, dh.ts, fifth.ts, pbta.ts, root.ts, su.ts in apps/discord-bot/src/commands/) total 663 LOC, matching the cited ~660. Each independently imports its game roll(), defines a buildXEmbed(), exports a Command with SlashCommandBuilder, and contains a byte-for-byte identical execute harness: parse options -> await deferReplyHonoringHidden(interaction) -> try { build embed; editReply } catch (e) { replyWithError(interaction, 'Error', e instanceof Error ? e.message : 'An unknown error occurred') }. That defer/try/catch/editReply block and the verbatim 'An unknown error occurred' string are duplicated 6x. No createGameCommand/factory exists today (grep confirms), and the Command interface in types.ts already supports the optional autocomplete (used only by su.ts) that a factory must preserve. The recommendation is sound and non-duplicative.

Adjusting severity P1 -> P2 because the finding overstates the blast radius. The extractable duplication is the ~10-line execute harness plus the error string (roughly ~60 LOC of true boilerplate), NOT the bulk of the 660 LOC. Most of each file is genuinely game-specific: buildXEmbed bodies differ substantially (blades 4-way resultConfig, dh advantage/disadvantage die handling, fifth nat-1/nat-20 detection, su a 20-entry color gradient) and the SlashCommandBuilder option definitions differ per game (dice 0-10 vs stat -3..5 vs modifier -30..30, plus su autocomplete). So the claim that each file drops to ~30 lines is optimistic; realistic per-file savings are ~10 lines. Additionally, this is a private, never-published internal app (per apps/discord-bot/CLAUDE.md), so a harness bug touching 6 files is real friction but bounded — no external consumers, nothing blocked. That profile is P2 (refine), not P1.

survivedFindings.title: Expo and RDN build systems diverge: Expo uses Metro (untyped JS, custom resolver), RDN uses Astro (standard Vite). No DRY pattern for workspace linking.
survivedFindings.problem: Expo's metro.config.js manually maps all @randsum/* subpaths to TypeScript source (e.g., @randsum/roller/roll -> ./packages/roller/src/roll.ts). If a new subpath is added, it must be manually added to Metro. The untyped Metro config is risky: changes don't surface in bun run typecheck. RDN and Site use Astro's native resolution, so they don't need custom mapping, but then they have slow builds if source files change (no TypeScript caching). No standard pattern exists.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Convert metro.config.js to metro.config.ts with explicit types, or generate it from a shared workspace-config.json. Document in CLAUDE.md: 'When adding a subpath, add it here and here.' For RDN/Site, consider whether importing TS source directly (like Metro) would speed up dev; if acceptable, leave as-is.

verdict.reasoning:
VERIFIED the core: apps/expo/metro.config.js (lines 26-53) hand-maintains a map of 7 @randsum/roller subpaths to TS source, mirroring exactly the 7 subpath exports in packages/roller/package.json (".", "/roll", "/errors", "/validate", "/tokenize", "/docs", "/trace"). The config is untyped JS — confirmed by apps/expo/CLAUDE.md, which itself flags both problems verbatim: "If a new subpath is added... it must be added to this map" and "metro.config.js is untyped JS. Changes there do not surface in bun run typecheck." So the genuine actionable defect is real and current: a hand-synced, typecheck-invisible duplication of roller's export surface that silently breaks native/web resolution if a subpath is added and forgotten. Not a duplicate — searched audit/ and plans/3.0.0-harsh-audit*.md; no prior coverage of Metro or build configs.

ADJUSTED because the finding overstates its scope in two ways. (1) The "build systems diverge / no DRY pattern" framing is weak: Metro (RN) and Astro (Vite) are different runtimes by necessity — Expo resolves to TS source specifically to skip an EAS prebuild for native bundles, while Astro/Vite resolve workspaces natively. This isn't arbitrary divergence. (2) The RDN claims are partly wrong: apps/rdn/astro.config.ts imports nothing from dice-ui and only build-time imports NOTATION_DOCS from roller (per rdn/CLAUDE.md), so "RDN has slow builds if source files change" is irrelevant; and apps/site/astro.config.mjs (line 188) ALREADY aliases @randsum/dice-ui to TS source via Vite, so a source-resolution pattern partially exists. The recommendation to convert to metro.config.ts is also mild — Metro loads JS configs by convention and .ts wouldn't gain typecheck coverage without extra wiring; the stronger fix is to derive the subpath map from roller's package.json#exports so it can never drift.

Net: legitimate P2 refine, but the actionable kernel is narrow (the untyped hand-synced Metro map), not the broad "build systems diverge" thesis. Severity P2 is correct.

survivedFindings.title: Expo lacks a 'check' script, making CI gate unclear
survivedFindings.problem: CLI, discord-bot, rdn, and site each define check: bun run build && bun run typecheck && ... But Expo has no check script. When a developer touches Expo code, it's ambiguous what verification to run. Root check doesn't include Expo, so untested changes might be pushed.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add check script to apps/expo/package.json: tsc --noEmit && eslint ... && prettier ... && bun test. Update root CLAUDE.md to document that Expo is excluded from root check due to Expo Router conventions, but developers must still run bun run --filter @randsum/expo test.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the actual files. The structural claim is TRUE: apps/cli, apps/discord-bot, apps/rdn, and apps/site each define a `check` script (`bun run build && bun run typecheck && bun run format:check && bun run lint && bun run test`), while apps/expo/package.json has no `check` and no `test` script (only start/android/ios/web/lint/format/typecheck). Root `check`/`check:all` in package.json explicitly enumerates roller, games, cli, discord-bot, site, rdn — expo is excluded.

However the finding is mis-framed on two points and needs adjustment:

1. "CI gate unclear" is partly wrong. .github/workflows/ci.yml DOES have a dedicated `expo` job (lines 166-178) that is part of the `ci-gate` needs array (line 263). So expo is gated in CI — it is not ungated. The job runs roller build + expo typecheck + expo lint + `bun test apps/expo/__tests__/IndexScreen.test.tsx`.

2. The recommendation is partially unsound as written: it suggests developers run `bun run --filter @randsum/expo test`, but no `test` script exists — I ran it and got "a package.json script 'test' was not found". The recommendation must first ADD a `test` script before it can be referenced.

Critically, the finding UNDERSTATES the real cost. The CI expo job cherry-picks exactly ONE of 11 test files (IndexScreen.test.tsx, which passes 12/12). The other 10 files are run by no gate. I ran the full suite (`bun test apps/expo/__tests__/`) and got 31 fail + 11 errors across 10 files (44 pass / 75 total). useRoll.ts shows 0% coverage. So the genuine problem is not merely "ambiguous what to verify" — it is a coverage hole hiding ~42 broken/erroring tests in a private app. The fix should add a `check` (and `test`) script AND have CI run the full expo suite, not one file. Severity P1 is reasonable (expo is private/unpublished, capping blast radius), but the category is closer to a real test-rot/coverage gap than a pure DX-clarity nit.

survivedFindings.title: Discord-bot builds with bunup but is never published to npm; unclear why it's in the same build pipeline as CLI
survivedFindings.problem: Discord-bot and CLI are built identically and included in the same root build command. But CLI is published to npm; discord-bot is private and deployed via GitHub Actions. They're in the same pipeline by cargo-cult inheritance, not because they share build concerns. This conflates 'build for publishing' with 'prepare for deployment'.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Separate concerns: (1) root build should only build publishable packages (roller, games, CLI). (2) Add separate CI jobs for app deployments, OR (3) create bun run build:all. Document: 'root build = publishable packages only; CI jobs build apps separately.'

verdict.reasoning:
PARTIALLY VERIFIED but mis-rated and mis-reasoned. Confirmed: root `build` (package.json:49) builds roller, games, cli, AND discord-bot; the release workflow (.github/workflows/publish.yml:30) runs that same root `bun run build` before publishing, so it does build the private discord-bot app needlessly during release. discord-bot is indeed private:true (apps/discord-bot/package.json:5).

However the finding's reasoning is partly WRONG and overstated:
(1) The claim 'discord-bot is deployed via GitHub Actions' is FALSE — there is no discord deploy workflow (.github/workflows/ has expo-native-deploy.yml and expo-web-deploy.yml but nothing for discord; discord deployment per apps/discord-bot/CLAUDE.md is entirely manual: build + deploy-commands + start).
(2) The 'publishing vs deployment conflation' is largely already solved: scripts/publish.ts uses a hardcoded PUBLISH_ORDER of only roller/games/cli and explicitly skips private packages, so discord-bot is never actually published — only built.
(3) CI is already well-separated: ci.yml gives each package its own path-filtered job (cli job lines 118-127 builds only roller+cli; discord-bot job lines 129-138 builds roller+games+discord-bot), so per-package concerns are NOT conflated in CI.

The only genuine residual issue is that the release workflow builds discord-bot unnecessarily — a few seconds of wasted CI time, no correctness impact. That is a P3 nitpick, not a P2 strategic concern. Recommendation (separate build:all from publishable-only build) is sound but low-value and partly redundant with the already-correct publish.ts filtering. Adjusting severity P2 -> P3.

survivedFindings.title: Root test script missing @randsum/dice-ui and @randsum/site
survivedFindings.problem: The root `test` script runs tests only for `@randsum/roller`, `@randsum/games`, `@randsum/cli`, `@randsum/discord-bot`, and `@randsum/rdn`. It excludes `@randsum/dice-ui` (packages/dice-ui) and `@randsum/site` (apps/site). If dice-ui or site tests fail, `bun run test` at root won't catch them, creating a CI/local test coverage gap. Per the audit's findings and per CI's knip job, these packages are part of the checked ecosystem.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add both packages to the test script: `bun run --filter '@randsum/roller' --filter '@randsum/games' --filter '@randsum/dice-ui' --filter '@randsum/cli' --filter '@randsum/discord-bot' --filter '@randsum/rdn' --filter '@randsum/site' test`

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is partly real, partly wrong, and its recommendation is unsound as written.

REAL part: `packages/dice-ui` is a genuine coverage gap. It has a real test file (`packages/dice-ui/__tests__/tokenColor.test.ts`) but its package.json (`packages/dice-ui/package.json`) defines only a `typecheck` script — there is NO `test` script. The root `test` script (package.json:50) omits dice-ui, and no CI job runs dice-ui's tests: in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` a change to `packages/dice-ui/**` (line 57) only triggers the `expo` job, which runs typecheck/lint plus the single file `apps/expo/__tests__/IndexScreen.test.tsx` — never `tokenColor.test.ts`. So that test genuinely never executes in CI or via root `bun run test`.

WRONG part: The finding claims `@randsum/site` is also excluded/uncovered. That is false. `apps/site/package.json` DOES have a `test` script (`bun test`), and its `check` script chains `... && bun run test`. CI runs `bun run --filter '@randsum/site' check` (ci.yml:151), and the site paths-filter (ci.yml:46-49) triggers on any `packages/**` or `apps/site/**` change. Site's 4 test files are covered. Adding site to the root test script would be redundant, not a fix.

UNSOUND recommendation: The proposed fix `--filter '@randsum/dice-ui' test` cannot work because dice-ui has no `test` script — bun would skip/error. The correct fix requires FIRST adding a `test` script (`bun test`) to `packages/dice-ui/package.json`, then either wiring it into root `test` or, better, into CI so it actually gates PRs (the root script is local-only; CI is per-package filtered).

SEVERITY: P1 overstates this. The single untested unit is `tokenColor.ts`, a UI color-mapping helper in a private (non-published) package; the failure-to-run risk is low-impact and isolated. P2 is appropriate.

Actionable, scoped-down recommendation: add `"test": "bun test"` to `packages/dice-ui/package.json`, then add a dice-ui CI job (or fold dice-ui test into the existing expo job's test step) so `tokenColor.test.ts` runs on `packages/dice-ui/**` changes. Drop the site portion of the finding entirely.

survivedFindings.title: check:all does not match CI pipeline — missing knip and size
survivedFindings.problem: The CLAUDE.md root section (line 28) says `bun run check:all` is the full CI pipeline. However, check:all runs only per-package `check` scripts (build, typecheck, format:check, lint, test). The actual CI also runs separate jobs for: `knip`, `size` per package, `gen:check`, and `conformance:check`. A developer running `bun run check:all` will see green and push, then CI fails on knip or bundle size. This contradicts CONTRIBUTING.md guidance.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Expand check:all to include: `&& bun run knip && bun run --filter '@randsum/roller' size && bun run --filter '@randsum/games' size` to match essential CI checks and catch failures locally before push.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed real and current. /Users/jarvis/Code/RANDSUM/@RANDSUM/package.json:57 defines `check:all` as `bun run check`, and `check` (line 56) only runs per-package `check` scripts = build + typecheck + format:check + lint + test (verified in packages/roller/package.json and packages/games/package.json). It does NOT run knip, size, or gen:check. Meanwhile .github/workflows/ci.yml runs knip as its own job (lines 252-258), `size` per package (lines 92, 116), and gen:check (line 105). The doc contradiction is accurate and arguably worse than stated: CLAUDE.md:28 literally claims `check:all` is the "Full CI pipeline (lint, format, typecheck, test, build, size, site)" — explicitly listing "size", which is false. CONTRIBUTING.md:17 and :63 tell contributors to run `check:all` to "Verify everything works" before pushing.

Two corrections to the finding's scope: (1) The pre-push lefthook hook (lefthook.yml:32-54) DOES run knip, gen:check, and conformance-check, so a developer pushing through git is caught on those items unless they use --no-verify. The finding overstates knip/gen:check as gaps a dev would actually hit. (2) The one genuinely unguarded item is `size` — it is absent from check:all AND from pre-push, so bundle-size regressions are only caught in CI. That is the real, concrete cost.

Note: MEMORY.md claims commit 6f663a3d added a size check to check:all, but current main's check:all (line 57) does not contain it — so it was either reverted or never landed on main. This finding is therefore not duplicative of already-shipped work; it is still open on main.

Severity adjusted P1->P2: this is doc-vs-script drift plus one missing local size check, not a correctness bug, and pre-push hooks already mitigate most listed items. Recommendation is sound (add `&& bun run knip && bun run --filter '@randsum/roller' size && bun run --filter '@randsum/games' size` to check:all) and, critically, CLAUDE.md:28's false "size" claim should be reconciled with whatever check:all actually does.

survivedFindings.title: Lefthook pre-push missing size check
survivedFindings.problem: Lefthook pre-push runs `build`, then `codegen-check`, `conformance-check`, `test`, `audit`, and `knip`, but does NOT run `size` checks. A developer can push code that increases bundle size beyond limits, and only CI will catch it post-push.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add a pre-push size check step after build (priority 1): `size: { run: "bun run --filter '@randsum/roller' size && bun run --filter '@randsum/games' size > /dev/null 2>&1", priority: 1 }`

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the actual files. lefthook.yml:32-54 shows the pre-push hook runs build (priority 1), then codegen-check, conformance-check, test, audit, and knip (all priority 2) — there is NO size check, exactly as described. The size limits are real CI gates: .github/workflows/ci.yml:91-116 and publish.yml:31-32 both run `bun run --filter '@randsum/roller' size` and `'@randsum/games' size`, confirming size violations are caught only post-push in CI. The per-package `size` script exists (invoked via --filter throughout CI), so the recommendation is implementable as written. Not a duplicate: the prior audit (audit/dimensions/build-tooling-ci.md) covered lefthook's parallel-install race (F6-ish) and pre-push test truncation (F7), but never the missing size check. So this is a genuinely new, still-open finding.

Two caveats keep this at P2 rather than higher: (1) CI is a reliable backstop, so the only cost is a slower feedback loop — a developer learns of a size regression after pushing rather than before, which is a shift-left convenience, not a correctness gap. (2) The recommendation's exact command is slightly inconsistent — it redirects only the games half to /dev/null while leaving roller's output visible, unlike the rest of the file which redirects whole commands; a cleaner form would wrap both. Bundle-size pushes are also relatively rare events. P2/refine is the correct rating; the finding is accurate and actionable.

survivedFindings.title: Root check script excludes @randsum/dice-ui
survivedFindings.problem: The root `check` script lists roller, games, cli, discord-bot, site, and rdn, but omits dice-ui. This means `bun run check` won't run dice-ui's typecheck, lint, format, or tests. Dice-ui is a workspace library used by site. Any type errors will only be caught by the full knip job, not by check:all.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add `&& bun run --filter '@randsum/dice-ui' check` to the root check command after the roller check.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed the core problem at the cited location. package.json:56 `check` runs per-package `check` for roller, games, cli, discord-bot, site, rdn and omits @randsum/dice-ui. dice-ui exists at packages/dice-ui, is consumed via workspace:~ by BOTH apps/site and apps/expo (finding only named site), and contains real .tsx source plus a live test file (packages/dice-ui/__tests__/tokenColor.test.ts). Since `check:all` -> `check` calls per-package check (not the root `@randsum/*` typecheck glob), dice-ui's typecheck never runs in the main gate and its test never runs anywhere in CI — a genuine gap.\n\nBUT the recommendation as written is unsound: dice-ui's package.json defines ONLY a `typecheck` script (no check/lint/format/test). I verified that `bun run --filter '@randsum/dice-ui' check` errors with "No packages matched the filter" because no `check` script exists. So appending `&& bun run --filter '@randsum/dice-ui' check` would break the chain, not fix it. The real fix is two-part: first add a `check` (and lint/format/test) script to packages/dice-ui/package.json matching the roller/rdn pattern (`build && typecheck && format:check && lint && test`, minus build since dice-ui has no build), THEN wire it into the root `check`. Severity P2 stands (private package, but an orphaned test and unlinted .tsx source is a real DX/CI hole). Adjusting because the problem is real and accurately located but the proposed one-liner is incorrect/incomplete.

survivedFindings.title: ESLint config ignores generated .ts files but treats them as entry points
survivedFindings.problem: ESLint's ignores array (line 43) includes `'**/*.generated.ts'`, so linting is skipped for code-generated files. However, knip.json explicitly lists generated files as entry points (e.g., `'src/*.generated.ts'` in games). This ambiguity means codegen bugs won't surface as lint errors. The generated files are committed and represent the public API.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Either (a) remove `'**/*.generated.ts'` from ESLint ignores so generated code is linted (catches codegen bugs), or (b) keep it ignored and document in codegen.ts that generated files skip ESLint checks. Option (a) is safer.

verdict.reasoning:
The factual observations are accurate but the analysis and primary recommendation are unsound. VERIFIED FACTS: eslint.config.js:43 ignores `**/*.generated.ts`; knip.json:24 lists `src/*.generated.ts` as a knip entry; knip.json:48 also ignores `**/*.generated.ts`. Running `bunx eslint --no-ignore` on packages/games/src/blades.generated.ts and salvageunion.generated.ts produces 12 errors. Not previously reported in audit/ or plans/3.0.0-harsh-audit*.md.

WHY ADJUSTED, NOT CONFIRMED:
1. The "ambiguity" framing is wrong. Knip "entry" and ESLint "ignore" are not in conflict — they are different tools doing different jobs. Knip's entry declares generated files as graph roots so their EXPORTS aren't reported as unused; knip's own ignore (line 48) stops knip analyzing the files themselves. ESLint ignoring them is a separate, independent decision. There is no genuine tension between knip entries and ESLint ignores.
2. The claim "codegen bugs won't surface as lint errors" is misleading. I inspected the 12 errors: they are all stylistic type-aware rules — `@typescript-eslint/unified-signatures` and `no-unnecessary-condition` — firing on INTENTIONAL defensive codegen patterns (blades.generated.ts:19-43 emits `input?.rating !== undefined && typeof input?.rating === 'number'` and optional chaining the type-checker knows is redundant). These are false positives on safe defensive code, not codegen bugs.
3. Recommendation (a) — "remove from ignores, it's safer" — is actively wrong. It would inject 12+ CI-blocking errors, forcing either rule-disabling or making codegen emit less-defensive code, for zero correctness gain. Codegen correctness is already guarded by `gen:check` (freshness), `typecheck`, and per-game unit + fast-check property tests. Codegen already runs Prettier (codegen.ts:15-17), so formatting is covered.

SEVERITY: P2 overstates impact (no correctness or runtime cost; purely a documentation-clarity nit). Lower to P3. The only sound, actionable remedy is recommendation (b): add a one-line comment near eslint.config.js:43 (and/or in codegen.ts) noting generated files intentionally skip ESLint because they are Prettier-formatted and type-checked, and that type-aware rules produce false positives on defensive codegen. Reject recommendation (a) entirely.

survivedFindings.title: Prettier config includes TOML override but lefthook doesn't format TOML
survivedFindings.problem: The Prettier config has an override for `*.toml` files, but the lefthook pre-commit format glob `'*.{ts,tsx,js,json,md,astro,css}'` (line 20) doesn't include TOML. This means bunfig.toml won't be auto-formatted by pre-commit hooks, though `bun run format` at root would format it. Inconsistency: staged changes to TOML files won't be formatted by hooks.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Update the lefthook format glob to include TOML: `'*.{ts,tsx,js,json,md,astro,css,toml,yml,yaml}'`

verdict.reasoning:
The core claim is verifiable. .prettierrc:38-42 (verified) has a `*.toml` override, and lefthook.yml:20 (verified) format glob `"*.{ts,tsx,js,json,md,astro,css}"` excludes TOML (and YAML/YAML). TOML files do exist: ./bunfig.toml, apps/expo/bunfig.toml, apps/site/netlify.toml, apps/rdn/netlify.toml. So staged TOML changes are not auto-formatted by pre-commit hooks — that part is accurate.

However, the finding contains a factual error in its justification: it claims "`bun run format` at root would format it." Root `format` is `bun run --filter '@randsum/*' format`, which delegates to each package's `prettier --write .` scoped to that package directory. The repo-root `./bunfig.toml` lives in NO package directory, so it is formatted by neither the hook nor `bun run format`. Only the apps/* TOML files (which ARE inside @randsum/* package dirs running `prettier --write .`) are covered by `bun run format` but not by hooks.

Severity is overstated. The only TOML-specific prettier override is `singleQuote: false`, which is already prettier's TOML default, so the formatting delta is effectively nil. bunfig.toml/netlify.toml are config files touched very rarely, and CI `format:check` (per-package) would catch any apps/* drift. The actual cost is negligible — a true but trivial consistency nitpick, not a P2. The recommendation (extend the glob to include toml/yml/yaml) is sound and low-risk, but this belongs at P3.

survivedFindings.title: Pre-commit knip output hidden, making failures opaque
survivedFindings.problem: Pre-push knip runs with output redirected to /dev/null: `bun run knip > /dev/null 2>&1`. If knip fails, the developer won't see why the push was blocked. Pre-commit lint, by contrast, shows output.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Change to: `run: bun run knip` to display knip output when it fails, helping developers understand what was detected.

verdict.reasoning: Location is accurate: lefthook.yml:53 has `bun run knip > /dev/null 2>&1`. But the finding's central justification is false. It claims knip is uniquely opaque and contrasts it with pre-commit lint "showing output." In reality: (1) this is the pre-push hook, not pre-commit; (2) within pre-push, knip is NOT special — build (line 38), codegen-check (41), conformance-check (44), and audit (50) ALL use the identical `> /dev/null 2>&1` redirect. The redirect is a deliberate, consistent house style for the pre-push band, not an oversight on knip. The only command that surfaces stdout is `test` (line 47, via `tail -5`), intentionally. Moreover, lefthook's `output: [summary, failure]` config (line 33) means a knip failure IS reported by lefthook as a failed command with non-zero exit — the developer sees that knip blocked the push and can re-run `bun run knip` for detail; the redirect only hides knip's stdout, not the fact of failure. So the problem is far smaller than stated, and the recommendation to un-redirect only knip would create inconsistency with four sibling commands; doing it consistently is a debatable style choice, not a clear fix. Real-but-trivial with a flawed premise: drop from P2 to P3.

survivedFindings.title: Help script lists non-existent 'mcp:compile' commands
survivedFindings.problem: The help script lists `'mcp:compile'` and `'mcp:compile:all'` commands under 'MCP Server', but these do not exist in package.json scripts. This misleads developers.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Remove the MCP Server category from help.ts, or add the scripts to package.json if they're a planned feature. Given the project scope (dice-rolling), this is likely stale documentation.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly against the code. scripts/help.ts lines 36-38 list 'mcp:compile' and 'mcp:compile:all' under an 'MCP Server' category. The root package.json scripts (full list inspected: prepare, help, dev, build, test, ..., changeset, version-packages, publish) contain NO mcp scripts. A repo-wide grep for 'mcp:compile' returns ONLY the two lines in help.ts itself — the commands exist nowhere else. No MCP package or app exists (packages/ = dice-ui, games, roller). Running `bun run mcp:compile` would fail. Not a duplicate: prior audit dirs (audit/, plans/) have zero references to help.ts or mcp:compile. MEMORY confirms MCP server is only an aspirational 'post-launch feature gated behind publishing,' so these entries are premature. The recommendation (remove the category, or add scripts if planned) is sound. P2 is correct: this is dev-facing doc drift that wastes time but is non-blocking. Note: help.ts has additional drift beyond this finding — the Release category lists 'changeset:version'/'changeset:publish' but actual scripts are 'version-packages'/'publish' — confirming the file is stale more broadly, which strengthens (not weakens) the finding.

Deletion is confirmed via commits f74c323d (feat!: delete @randsum/display-utils, absorbed into @randsum/roller subpaths) and 60404f12. survivedFindings.title: llms.txt claims RDN v1.0 conformance when only v0.9.0 exists
survivedFindings.problem: llms.txt line 3 states 'RDN v1.0 Level 4 (Full) Conformant' but only v0.9.0 exists in /Users/jarvis/Code/RANDSUM/@RANDSUM/apps/rdn/src/content/specs/ (verified with 'ls'). survivedFindings.recommendation: Change 'RDN v1.0' to 'RDN v0.9.0' in llms.txt line 3. This aligns with actual spec availability in /apps/rdn/src/content/specs/v0.9.0.md.

verdict.reasoning:

The READMEs were rolled back; llms.txt is the leftover. Cost is contained entirely to LLM-context/documentation consistency with zero functional or runtime impact, one line in one non-published file.
