survivedFindings.title: README.md still advertises non-existent interactive TUI mode
survivedFindings.problem: Line 73 states 'npx @randsum/cli         # Interactive TUI mode'. However, the CLI (apps/cli/src/index.ts) is a one-shot notation parser with no interactive/render path — the TUI/ink code was removed in PR #1056 despite an earlier plan to keep it. Users who run 'npx @randsum/cli' with no args get help text, not a TUI. The audit memo confirms this was REMOVED, not implemented.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Remove the interactive TUI line entirely. The CLI now only supports: notation args, --verbose, --json, --repeat, --seed, and stdin/pipe mode. Example usage: 'npx @randsum/cli 2d20' or 'echo 4d6L | npx @randsum/cli'. Update example to reflect actual usage.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against actual code. README.md:73 reads `npx @randsum/cli         # Interactive TUI mode`. apps/cli/src/index.ts is a pure one-shot arg parser: with zero notations, main() prints HELP and process.exit(1) (lines 110-114). There is no interactive/render/ink path in index.ts, run.ts, or format.ts; apps/cli/CLAUDE.md itself describes it as a "Simple terminal dice roller. Pass notation args, get results." So the README advertises a non-existent mode, and the actual behavior (help text + exit 1) directly contradicts it. Location and recommendation are accurate; removing the line and using a real example (e.g. `echo 4d6L | npx @randsum/cli` for stdin mode, supported at index.ts:104-108) is sound and non-duplicative. One refinement strengthening the finding: README.md:33 also falsely claims "@randsum/cli - CLI and interactive TUI for rolling dice" — the same fix should cover both occurrences. Severity P1 kept: it is the project's canonical README, top-level Quick Example, advertising an entry experience that doesn't exist; though doc-only (not a functional/data bug), it is user-facing and appears in two places, so P1 stands rather than dropping to P2.

survivedFindings.title: RDN version string hardcoded across multiple app READMEs
survivedFindings.problem: RDN conformance claim 'RDN v0.9.0 Level 4 (Full) Conformant' is duplicated in at least 3 files: root README, cli/README, discord-bot/README. No single source of truth. If RDN is bumped to v1.0, all three files must be updated manually, increasing risk of drift.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Extract the RDN version claim to a shared constant in llms.txt or a dedicated config. Alternatively, add it to root CLAUDE.md as the canonical statement, then reference 'See root CLAUDE.md' in app READMEs. Verify this doesn't require CI-build-time substitution (should be static doc text, not generated).

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED the underlying problem but the finding undercounts the scope and is partly duplicative of prior audit F8. The RDN conformance string is hardcoded in 6 files, not 3: README.md:17, packages/roller/README.md:18, packages/games/README.md:5, apps/cli/README.md:10, apps/discord-bot/README.md:5 (all "RDN v0.9.0 Level 4 (Full) Conformant"), and llms.txt:3 ("RDN v1.0 Level 4 (Full) Conformant"). The finding's location ("cli, discord-bot") and count ("at least 3 files") are both understated.

The DRY risk the finding predicts has ALREADY MATERIALIZED: llms.txt says v1.0 while all five READMEs say v0.9.0, while the canonical spec (apps/rdn/src/content/specs/v0.9.0.md, the only spec file, Status: Draft) is v0.9.0. So llms.txt is making a false conformance claim to a spec version that does not exist as a published document. This is a live correctness/credibility defect, not a hypothetical.

This is substantially duplicative of the prior audit's F8 (audit/dimensions/apps.md:76-81, rated P1): "Only one spec version (v0.9.0 Draft); READMEs claim 'RDN v1.0 Level 4 Conformant'." The audit commit 6cf8b298 applied F8's option (b) — changing the five READMEs to v0.9.0 — but missed llms.txt, which still carries the stale v1.0 claim. So the genuinely-NEW, still-open item is: llms.txt:3 was not updated in the F8 fix and now contradicts every README.

Adjusting because: (1) the finding's framing (pure DRY/shared-constant refactor, effort M) overweights the engineering solution for a one-line static doc string — a shared-constant/build-substitution layer is overkill; (2) the real, actionable defect is the single stale line in llms.txt, fixable in seconds, which the finding buries; (3) scope/location are inaccurate (6 files, including root/roller/games READMEs the finding omits). Severity stays P2: a false conformance claim in llms.txt (consumed by LLM agents as authoritative) is more than cosmetic, but it is one line and low blast radius.

Actionable fix: change llms.txt:3 from "RDN v1.0 Level 4" to "RDN v0.9.0 Level 4" to match the published spec and the five READMEs. A shared-constant extraction is optional and not worth M effort for one string.

survivedFindings.title: game-spec-creator skill lacks end-to-end walkthrough example
survivedFindings.problem: The game-spec-creator skill provides excellent spec format documentation (lines 19-194) but doesn't include a **complete worked example** of: (1) creating a new .randsum.json, (2) running 'bun run --filter @randsum/games gen' to generate code, (3) adding a subpath export to package.json, (4) writing and running tests. New contributors may generate a spec but not know the complete workflow. The references/SPEC_EXAMPLES.md file has spec examples but no instructions on what to do after creating them.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Add a 'Full Walkthrough' section to SKILL.md (after section 'Common Patterns') that walks through: (a) creating a simple blades-style game spec, (b) placing it at packages/games/mygame.randsum.json, (c) running codegen, (d) adding subpath export, (e) writing a unit test, (f) running bun run --filter @randsum/games test. Alternatively, link to CONTRIBUTING.md#Adding-a-New-Game which already covers this.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the actual files. The core claim is partially accurate: /Users/jarvis/Code/RANDSUM/@RANDSUM/skills/game-spec-creator/SKILL.md focuses on spec authoring and its "Validation and Testing" section (lines 184-189) mentions codegen and tests but omits the integration steps — adding the subpath export to package.json, the size-limit entry, and file placement. So there is a genuine gap relative to a full end-to-end walkthrough.

However, the finding overstates both severity and novelty. The complete workflow is already documented and discoverable in TWO places the finding itself references: CONTRIBUTING.md "Adding a New Game" (lines 27-36) and, more completely, packages/games/CLAUDE.md "Adding a New Game" (lines 87-98), which gives a precise 6-step workflow including the subpath-export gotcha (exports-sync.test.ts drift guard) and size-limit entry. The skill also already links to references/SPEC_EXAMPLES.md for full specs. The skill's stated scope (its frontmatter description: "create .randsum.json specs / understand the RANDSUM spec format") is spec authoring, not the package-integration lifecycle — so the missing steps are arguably out of scope by design.

Minor inaccuracy in the finding: it proposes placing the spec at "packages/games/mygame.randsum.json", but the real convention is the package root with the shortcode name (`<shortcode>.randsum.json`), and tests live at packages/games/__tests__/. The recommendation to add a walkthrough OR link to the existing workflow doc is sound and non-duplicative of code, but the lower-effort, higher-value fix is simply a one-line cross-link from SKILL.md's "Validation and Testing" section to packages/games/CLAUDE.md#Adding-a-New-Game (the canonical source), not authoring a parallel M-effort walkthrough that would risk drift from the real docs.

Net: real but mis-rated. This is a low-cost doc nicety (workflow is one grep away in two discoverable docs), so P3 not P2, and the effort should be S (add a cross-link), not M.

survivedFindings.title: CLI published to npm but under-feature-promoted and possibly mis-scoped
survivedFindings.problem: The CLI is published as @randsum/cli (6 versions on npm) and documented in the root README, but its README is only 50 lines and focuses on single notation rolls. The tool is positioned as 'a simple terminal dice roller,' but the README hints at features that don't exist (interactive mode was removed per audit #1056) or are undocumented (stdin/pipe support was added but not mentioned in the README). A user npx @randsum/cli expecting a TUI or REPL gets a one-shot notation parser, which feels incomplete. The README doesn't explain the intended use case: quick ad-hoc rolls from the terminal, or a game aid that runs alongside a TTRPG session.
survivedFindings.recommendation: Clarify the CLI's positioning: either (1) promote it as a lightweight one-shot tool with stdin support documented (echo '4d6L' | @randsum/cli), or (2) invest in building a simple TUI mode with multi-roll sessions, history, and template support. Update the README to show the stdin pattern, JSON output option, and example one-liners for game integration. If TUI is genuinely out of scope, remove 'Interactive TUI' from the root README's feature list.

verdict.reasoning:
Partly confirmed, partly refuted — the actionable doc-drift is real but the finding mis-locates it and includes a stale recommendation.

CONFIRMED (real & current):
1. The CLI is now a pure one-shot notation roller. The TUI was deliberately REMOVED — apps/cli/src has only index.ts, run.ts, format.ts; there is NO tui/ dir, and apps/cli/package.json no longer depends on ink or dice-ui.
2. The ROOT README (/Users/jarvis/Code/RANDSUM/@RANDSUM/README.md) still falsely advertises the removed TUI: line 33 ("CLI and interactive TUI for rolling dice") and line 73 ("npx @randsum/cli  # Interactive TUI mode"). Running `npx @randsum/cli` with no args + a TTY now just prints help and exits 1 — no TUI. This is a genuine, current doc-drift bug the prior audit's item #14 did NOT catch (that item was about the *CLI* README's phantom `-i` flag, which is now fixed).
3. stdin/pipe support exists in code (index.ts readStdinSync + !process.stdin.isTTY) but apps/cli/README.md never documents the `echo '4d6L' | randsum` pattern. Confirmed.

REFUTED / MIS-LOCATED:
- The finding blames apps/cli/README.md for the phantom interactive/TUI claim. That README is clean — it has NO interactive/TUI mention and correctly documents --json, -r, -s, etc. The phantom-TUI text is in the ROOT README.md, not the cited file.
- Recommendation option (2) "invest in building a simple TUI mode" is stale/refuted: the TUI was just deliberately removed (and ink mis-classification was flagged in the prior audit). Re-adding it contradicts the current direction. Only the documentation-cleanup half of the recommendation (document stdin, strip 'Interactive TUI' from root README) is sound and non-duplicative.

Net: real, low-severity documentation drift. Adjusting because the cited location is wrong (root README.md, not apps/cli/README.md) and half the recommendation is unsound. Keeping P2 is reasonable but I'd lean P3 — it's pure doc cleanup with no functional/publish impact. Marking P3.

survivedFindings.title: sideEffects: true on roller prevents tree-shaking, shipping full engine when consumers only need tokenize
survivedFindings.problem: The roller's package.json sets sideEffects: true, which tells bundlers to pull the full dist/index.js even if a consumer imports only @randsum/roller/tokenize. Per CLAUDE.md, this was reverted from false due to a bunup 0.16.31 issue with subpath re-exports, but the issue is never cited or explained. Without proper tree-shaking, every UI component that uses tokenize pulls the full modifier registry, random number generation, and roll engine (~16 KB of dead code).
survivedFindings.recommendation: Investigate the underlying bunup issue with subpath re-exports and either: (1) upgrade bunup if the issue is fixed in 0.17+, or (2) split the roller into src/tokenize-only and src/full-engine with explicit entry points so sideEffects: false works. If the re-export issue persists, document it in CLAUDE.md as a known limitation and add a GitHub issue linking to the bunup tracker. This is a high-value win for UI bundle sizes.

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL but the finding's framing and recommendation are wrong and partly duplicative; severity unchanged at P2.

Core problem is real and current. packages/roller/package.json line 6 is `"sideEffects": true`. The main barrel dist/index.js re-exports the entire engine via shared chunks (verified: roll, modifiers, notation, validation all re-exported), so a consumer importing a narrow surface from `@randsum/roller` (e.g. `import { isDiceNotation } from '@randsum/roller'`) cannot tree-shake the roll engine out. This is a measured ~37 KB consumer cost (51.3 KB vs 14.5 KB).

KEY CORRECTION the finding missed: this is a REGRESSION from an Accepted ADR, not an unexplained workaround. Git history shows the correct value was `"sideEffects": ["./src/**/*"]` (commit 8d7f6d28, documented in docs/adr/ADR-018-roller-sideeffects-src-glob.md, Status: Accepted). The src-glob matches nothing at consumer time (published `files` is dist/LICENSE/README only — src/ is never shipped) so it tree-shakes like `false`, while matching at BUILD time to sidestep bunup 0.16.31's self-DCE bug that breaks dist/docs/index.js and dist/trace/index.js when `false` is used. During the 30-item audit a dev tried `false` (commit e4163b0a), hit the documented bunup bug, and INCORRECTLY reverted to `true` instead of restoring the working `["./src/**/*"]` array. The audit merge (6cf8b298) carried `true` forward.

WHERE THE FINDING IS WRONG (hence adjusted, not confirmed):
1. It claims the bunup issue is 'never cited or explained' — false. ADR-018 documents it exhaustively (alternatives tested, measurements, references) and commit e4163b0a's message explains the exact failure (broken minified subpath output).
2. It mis-describes the cost: consumers importing the dedicated subpath `@randsum/roller/tokenize` are NOT affected — that is a separate bunup entry point producing an isolated 256-byte dist/tokenize.js. The real cost is narrow imports from the main barrel `@randsum/roller`. CLAUDE.md's tokenize-isolation invariant relies on separate entry points + ESM tree-shaking, not the sideEffects field.
3. Its recommendation (investigate/upgrade bunup, split tokenize vs full-engine entry points, file a GH issue) is over-engineered and duplicates work already done. The correct, already-validated, one-line fix is: restore `"sideEffects": ["./src/**/*"]` per Accepted ADR-018 (which yields the measured 72% reduction, keeps all size-limit budgets and tests green, requires no bunup upgrade or entry-point split). bunup is still 0.16.31, so `false` remains unsafe.

Severity P2 (refine) is appropriate: real build/DX hygiene regression affecting downstream UI bundle sizes, but not release-blocking.

Recommended action: change packages/roller/package.json line 6 from `"sideEffects": true` to `"sideEffects": ["./src/**/*"]`, restoring conformance to ADR-018. Verify with `bun run --filter @randsum/roller size` and the dist smoke test.
